front: What is the formal definition of an ARMA(p, q) process?
back: An ARMA(p, q) process {X_t} is defined by the equation: X_t = φ₁X_{t-1} + ... + φ_pX_{t-p} + Z_t + θ₁Z_{t-1} + ... + θ_qZ_{t-q}, where {Z_t} is a white noise process with mean zero and variance σ².

front: What are the two pure components that make up a mixed ARMA model, and what do their orders (p and q) represent?
back: The two components are the Autoregressive (AR) part of order p and the Moving Average (MA) part of order q. 'p' represents the number of past values of the series used in the model, and 'q' represents the number of past white noise terms used.

front: What is the key assumption for stationarity in an ARMA process, and which part of the model does it apply to?
back: The process is stationary if the roots of the autoregressive characteristic polynomial φ(z) = 1 - φ₁z - ... - φ_pz^p lie outside the unit circle (|z| > 1). This condition applies only to the AR portion of the model.

front: What is meant by the 'invertibility' of an ARMA process, and which part of the model must satisfy this condition?
back: Invertibility means the process can be represented as a convergent pure AR(∞) process. It requires that the roots of the moving average characteristic polynomial θ(z) = 1 + θ₁z + ... + θ_qz^q lie outside the unit circle. This condition applies to the MA portion.

front: Why are mixed ARMA models often more parsimonious than pure AR or MA models?
back: An ARMA model can often achieve a good fit to data with fewer total parameters (p + q) than a pure AR or MA model would require alone. A low-order ARMA(p, q) model can approximate the behavior of a much higher-order AR or MA model.
